sentences.text: MSHA Safety Alert — Electric Arc Flashes (Official Text) In the last six months, 11 miners received injuries due to electric arc flashes.
Best Practices — MSHA Required Checklist Establish safe procedures before beginning work and discuss the plan with all miners involved in the task.
Electricians must know how to de-energize and disconnect electrical systems and equipment.
Use properly rated electrical meters and personal protective equipment, such as electrically rated gloves, insulated blankets or mats, and polycarbonate barriers.
Lock out and tag out circuits before working on electrical equipment: Identify and control all hazardous energy sources before conducting any task.
Open the circuit breaker or load break switch to de-energize the incoming power cables or conductors.
Open the visual disconnect to confirm incoming power is off.
Department of Labor, Mine Safety and Health Administration MSHA Training Videos.
MSHA's official video library covering electrical and other surface/underground mining hazards, used across the industry for required refresher training.
Debrief: Notice the time scale — fractions of a second from fault to full blast.
There is no "pull your hand back" reaction time.
The only effective protection is preventing the arc from initiating at all (de-energized work, proper procedure) and PPE rated for the available incident energy when energized work is unavoidable.

MSHA's own data shows electricians and mechanics account for the majority of arc flash injuries in mining, but preparation/plant workers and other trades are also represented — proximity to the work, not just job title, drives the risk.
